introSection: Aconcagua was a Schwarzkopf roller coaster that operated at Parque de la Ciudad from February 1983 to 10 November 2003.

sections.title: Ride
sections.content:
When the roller coaster begins, the train slowly exits the station and engages with the chain lift hill that carries it to the top of the structure, reaching a height of approximately 36 meters, which at the time made it the tallest roller coaster in South America. From this point, riders have a panoramic view of the park and the city skyline.

At the peak, the train is released and drops down a steep first descent, quickly accelerating to speeds close to 90 kilometers per hour. After this initial plunge, the train climbs a smaller hill that creates moments of airtime before continuing into a series of broad turns and gentle undulations, maintaining speed and generating moderate lateral forces.

Next, the train navigates a second, shorter drop followed by a combination of straight sections and wide curves that sustain the feeling of continuous motion. Finally, the ride concludes with a final brake run, where the train gradually slows down before returning to the station.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: 3 trains with 7 cars per train. Riders are arranged 2 across in 2 rows for a total of 28 riders per train.

fullText:
Aconcagua was a Schwarzkopf roller coaster that operated at Parque de la Ciudad from February 1983 to 10 November 2003.



When the roller coaster begins, the train slowly exits the station and engages with the chain lift hill that carries it to the top of the structure, reaching a height of approximately 36 meters, which at the time made it the tallest roller coaster in South America. From this point, riders have a panoramic view of the park and the city skyline.

At the peak, the train is released and drops down a steep first descent, quickly accelerating to speeds close to 90 kilometers per hour. After this initial plunge, the train climbs a smaller hill that creates moments of airtime before continuing into a series of broad turns and gentle undulations, maintaining speed and generating moderate lateral forces.

Next, the train navigates a second, shorter drop followed by a combination of straight sections and wide curves that sustain the feeling of continuous motion. Finally, the ride concludes with a final brake run, where the train gradually slows down before returning to the station.

3 trains with 7 cars per train. Riders are arranged 2 across in 2 rows for a total of 28 riders per train.
